Top 10 Best things to do in tokyo
videos.transcript: In this video, we'll show you the top 10 things to do in Tokyo. The suggestions are based on our fun trip to the largest city on Earth. Don't forget to like this video, subscribe to our channel, and enable notifications. And share your own experience in the comments below. We want to thank Ceptics, the number one choice for travel adapters on Amazon, for sponsoring this video. The link is in the description. And stick around until the end of this video because we have a bonus for you. Here are our top 10 picks: Number 10: Takeshita Street Tokyo is famous for its diverse shopping options, including luxury fashion brands, electronics, and traditional Japanese goods. Explore Takeshita Street in Harajuku, known for its trendy fashion, unique boutiques, and youth culture. The area is known for its vibrant street style. It is often associated with the "Harajuku girls" who dress in eclectic and colorful fashion and the busy multilevel Tokyu Plaza Omotesando Harajuku shopping mall. Try the rainbow toast – because why not :) the longest tunnel potato, or one of many other delicious desserts, like cotton candy at Totti Candy Factory or Marion crêpe. If you're up for a unique cute experience, don't skip the nearby Hedgehog café. And there are tons of places all over Tokyo where you can experience a culture of cuteness or, as they call it in Japan – kawaii. There are many other districts and neighborhoods that offer unique shopping experiences, like the upscale Ginza, known for its luxury shopping, high-end fashion boutiques, and department stores. Number 9: Sensō-ji Temple Japan has two major religions: Shinto and Buddhism. And the two that have coexisted for centuries. While both Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples in Japan are places of worship and can be visited by the general public, they have different customs and practices associated with them. Did you know there are over 3,000 shrines and temples in Tokyo, more than any other city in Japan? Visit Sensō-ji, one of Tokyo's most popular and oldest Buddhist temples. Although rebuilt several times over the centuries due to fires and other natural disasters, and bombing during the World War II it is still known for its rich history, traditional architecture, and cultural significance. It dates back to the 7th century when two fishermen discovered a Kannon statue in the Sumida River. The temple was built around the statue, now housed in the main hall. One of the most striking features of Sensō-ji is the temple's main gate, the Kaminarimon, which is adorned by a large paper lantern bearing the temple's name. Visitors to the temple typically walk through the gate and down the Nakamise-dōri, a shopping street lined with traditional shops selling souvenirs and Japanese goods, leading to the temple's main hall. The temple also features several other buildings, including a pagoda, a treasure house, and a museum. Don't forget to explore the rest of Asakusa with several shopping streets and delicious food. Number 8: Food Tokyo is one of the world's top destinations for foodies. It offers a diverse and delicious food scene and a wide variety of cuisines and dining experiences, like the intriguing alleys called yokocho, for example the famous Omoide Yokocho, aka Memory Lane - a narrow street filled with tiny bars and restaurants or, as the Japanese called it, izakayas or for instance, Golden Gai or Golden Block, both popular nightlife spots, and both located in Shinjuku. The area is known for its bohemian atmosphere and association with the city's counterculture and nightlife. Of course, while visiting these unique places, get a taste of Japanese rice wine called sake, often served as part part of a traditional Japanese meal. You can enjoy it as an aperitif or paired with various dishes. Tokyo – the city with the most Michelin-starred restaurants in the world is considered one of the world's food capitals Besides its famous typical dishes like sushi and its many variations of sushi, like sashimi and nigiri, or renowned ramen, udon, or soba. Don't skip delicious street food with famous takoyaki octopus balls, stir-fried noodles called yakisoba, skewered rice dumplings named dango, and many others. Japan is also known for its delicious and unique desserts, many of which have been influenced by traditional Japanese ingredients and techniques, for example, Wagashi that comes in various shapes and flavors and is often enjoyed with tea, like mochi, or other desserts, like dorayaki – a type of Japanese pancake or fish-shaped dessert called taiyaki. Check our travel guide for more suggestions and ideas about which cafes to visit. By the way, our mobile-friendly travel guide covers the top 20 things to do in Tokyo and things to know before you visit, including maps, opening hours, links, itinerary suggestions, best day trips, and other information. Number 7: Tokyo Imperial Palace The Imperial Palace in Tokyo is the main residence of the Emperor of Japan located in Chiyoda, It is located on the former site of Edo Castle (Chiyoda Castle) a large feudal castle built in the mid-15th century. The castle was the shogun's residence and military government headquarters throughout the Edo period. Did you know that Tokyo was originally a small fishing village known as Edo, which means "estuary" in Japanese? In 1868, following the Meiji Restoration, the capital was moved from Kyoto and the city's name was changed to Tokyo meaning "Eastern Capital," to reflect its new status as the capital of the modernized Japan. However, Edo Castle has a long history with several fires occurring over the centuries. The last major one in 1873, destroyed much of the castle. Today, only a few structures from the original castle remain, including the castle's foundation stones, moats, and walls. In 1960, a palace called the Imperial Palace was built on the site of the former Edo Castle, which now serves as the residence of the emperor of Japan. You can visit the palace on a guided tour or freely walk around the gardens and the ruins. Discover the enchanting Chiyoda City, an idyllic destination filled with picturesque canals, vibrant gardens, and charming pathways, like The East Gardens of the Imperial Palace. During the Sakura season, make sure to rent a boat at the moat and immerse yourself in the breathtaking beauty of cherry blossoms. Number 6: Vending machines Vending machines are a common sight in Japan. They can be found in many locations, including on the street, train stations, and shopping malls. They offer various products with drinks, snacks, and unique items, like a cake in a can, ramen, or even insects. Insert some coins and select from a diverse array of cartoon characters, anime stars, and collectibles available in vinyl, plushies, and other forms. It is estimated that with 5 million machines, Japan has the highest number of vending machines per capita – literally one for every 25 people. Vending machines are popular due to the country's high population density and busy lifestyle, which makes purchasing items from vending machines a quick and convenient option for people. One of the most interesting ones is the so-called Horror Vending Machine Corner, located close to Akihabara. Number 5: Miraikan Museum Tokyo's museums offer a diverse and fascinating array of exhibits, showcasing everything from traditional Japanese art and culture to innovative technology and contemporary art worldwide. Miraikan, also known as The National Museum of Emerging Science and Innovation, is a cutting-edge museum offering diverse exhibits and experiences showcasing the latest advancements in science and technology. Visitors are immersed in interactive displays, virtual reality simulations, and hands-on experiments, providing a unique opportunity to explore the frontiers of innovation. Miraikan covers a wide array of topics, including robotics, space exploration, artificial intelligence, and environmental sustainability. It serves as a hub for scientific education and exploration, encouraging visitors to ponder the impact of emerging technologies on society and the world we inhabit. Miraikan stands as a beacon of scientific discovery, inspiring curiosity and fostering a deeper understanding of the rapidly evolving world of science and technology. Tokyo offers an array of museums to explore, making it an ideal destination, especially on rainy days. Number 4: Shibuya Crossing Shibuya Crossing, or "Scramble Crossing," is a busy intersection in the heart of the commercial and financial center - Shibuya. It is known for its large crowds of pedestrians crossing the street simultaneously, creating a unique, almost chaotic scene. The crossing is located in front of the Shibuya Station. It is often featured in movies and TV shows as a symbol of Tokyo's bustling city life. It is considered one of the busiest pedestrian crossings in the world, with as many as 2,500 people crossing at one time during peak hours. Shibuya is a popular tourist destination, and many people come to watch the crossing from the surrounding buildings. This place is scheduled for redevelopment in the near future. Don't skip a popular meeting spot at The Hachikō Memorial Statue honoring the loyal Akita dog Hachikō, who waited for his deceased owner at the train station every day for nine years. Explore the rest of Shibuya, like the narrow street of Shibuya Center-Gai, which is especially popular among youths. It offers many food options, brand-name stores, and busy nightlife. This video is sponsored by Ceptics. Being stuck in a hotel with only one outlet and many devices waiting to be charged can become a problem Use powerful travel adapters with USB, USB-C, and other plugs from Ceptis for a quick charge. Some even offer the ability to charge 7 devices simultaneously. Did you know different adapters charge your phone, laptop, and other devices at different speeds? Sometimes charging goes nowhere, and you need your devices ready to go fast when traveling. Japan uses the same outlet style as the US, but if you are traveling to other destinations in Asia, for instance, Singapore uses an entirely different outlet style - your Ceptics adapter can quickly be converted to fit the outlet style used on your next destination. You can even buy a voltage converter for small electronics like a toothbrush, shaver, and straightener that you otherwise couldn't use. Visit the Ceptics website to find adapters that fit your needs. The link is in the description. Number 3: Ueno Park Ueno Park, established in 1873, is a large public park known for museums, temples, gardens, and a zoo. The park is home to several important cultural institutions, including the Tokyo National Museum and the National Museum of Nature and Science. The park also features several temples, like Benten-do Temple, dedicated to the goddess of mercy. One of the main attractions in Ueno Park is the Shinobazu Pond, home to various bird species. Rent a boat and enjoy beautiful views of the surrounding area. Tokyo is home to many beautiful parks and gardens. Check our travel guide for more suggestions. Number 2: Otaku culture Otaku culture is a significant part of Japanese pop culture and has also gained popularity around the world. This subculture is associated with strong interest in anime, manga, video games, and other forms of popular entertainment. The term "otaku" originally referred to people with obsessive interests but has since taken on a more positive connotation. Otaku culture encompasses various attractions and activities, including cosplay, collectible card games, and fan fiction. It is also known for its vibrant subcultures, such as the maid cafés, where young women dress in maid costumes and serve customers in a café setting. Tokyo is home to several places popular among otaku culture fans, such as the famous Akihabara neighborhood, often referred to as the "otaku capital" of Japan, and Ghibli Museum, dedicated to the works of the Japanese animation studio. Number 1: Observation decks Tokyo offers several impressive observation decks that offer panoramic views of the city. Visit at least The Tokyo Skytree, a broadcasting and observation tower completed in 2012. At 2,080 feet (634 meters) it is the tallest structure in Japan. The Skytree features observation decks, a restaurant, and a shopping mall. The tower is designed to withstand earthquakes and typhoons. Don't skip The Tokyo Tower - a 1092 ft (333 meters) tall communications and observation tower built in 1958, once the tallest structure in Japan until the completion of the Tokyo Skytree in 2012. The tower is a popular tourist destination and is often used as a symbol of Tokyo. If you don't have enough time to visit one of two observation decks, just walk around the Zojo-ji Buddhist temple with beautiful views towards Tokyo Tower. And here is the bonus that we promised. If you have extra time in Tokyo, visit one of many unique cafes. There is no place like Tokyo when it comes to exciting bars and restaurants like Avatar Robot Café. Experience a one-of-a-kind dining adventure or enjoy refreshing juice, where you'll be served by robot avatars controlled remotely by individuals with disabilities or mental health challenges, enabling them to engage with society and overcome the limitations of their homebound circumstances. We wish you an amazing trip to Tokyo.

videos.transcript: If you've already been to Tokyo, you've probably seen all the main attractions, and now you want something different. Well, here are 20 things in and around Tokyo that you've probably never heard of, all accessible in a day trip or less with public transport. This video is sponsored by Tokyo Tourism, who suggested most of these locations, but I'm only including ones that I personally approve of. Kicking off the list, we've got a place that I've been to a couple of times now in different seasons, and it's absolutely beautiful. That is Togakushi Shrine. This place is stunning — just the trees and the vibes. In all weather, it's going to look amazing, and in different seasons, it's completely different but so, so, so magical. There's a bus that can drop you off at any of the main points along the way, or you can follow my suggestion, which is to get off at Hokosha bus stop. Then there's like a three-hour mini pilgrimage kind of hike that is just so beautiful. It visits the five main shrines of Togakushi. In winter, the hike won't be accessible, though, because there's just way too much snow. But you can still get off the bus at any of the stops along the way, especially the first one and the last one. I highly recommend them! For the final one in winter, again, don't go past the Cedar Corridor because there'll just be too much snow, and you won't be able to access the last shrine. But the corridor itself is absolutely beautiful, and as a bonus, there'll be nobody else there. To get here, catch a train to Nagano Station and from there, catch a bus for about one hour to Hokosha bus stop. Also, this is in my travel guide! Okay, next up, we have Zenkoji and its surrounding area. This one is also right next to Nago Station, so you could bundle it together with the Togakushi hike, or you could just do it separately on its own. This temple is massive and really, really beautiful. There's actually a number of different things to see in the area around it. The thing that I really love is this lined path all the way down towards the temple, and there are heaps of all these really cute, really interesting little shops and restaurants. The one that comes to mind in particular is a shop where you can make your own Japanese shichimi, like hot pepper powder. You get to choose all the different flavors that you like and then put it together with the right ratios and everything. Then, you get to take home your own personalized souvenir hot pepper. I really, really loved this experience — like surprisingly loved it — and it was super affordable as well. I think it only cost me like 800 yen or something. The other interesting thing that we did was we ate some Shin Yori, which, if you don't already know, is a Japanese monk's cuisine. It's very traditional, and there are a bunch of rules that they need to follow when they make it. Like, everything has to have at least five different colors and five different flavors. Honestly, I thought I knew what kind of food we would be eating, but it blew away all of my expectations, and I really, really, really enjoyed it. I highly recommend this to everyone, even if you're not vegan or vegetarian. Oh yes, it's traditionally vegan cuisine — I should have mentioned that. Even if you're not vegan, it's so tasty and so unique, and I highly recommend this. Also, to get here, just take a bus from Nago Station about 14 minutes to Zenkoji bus stop. So this next location is one that feels and sounds totally inauthentic. It sounds like one of those wow, crazy Japan tourist traps, but it's actually 100% real. This is Vowz Bar, and simply put, it is run by monks. They're real, practicing monks. That's a real shrine in the corner, and they're serving you real alcoholic drinks. The drinks themselves are really tasty, quite unique, and have really fun names as well in the menu. The food they serve, like the bar snacks and stuff, is all shoji ryori, which I just talked about — that's the traditional monks' cuisine. A couple of times throughout the night, they'll host this mini Buddhism sermon in Japanese and in English. They'll also invite you to partake in chanting the sutra. Overall, it's just a really fun, really wholesome experience, and it's really easy to get to as well. You just need to catch the train to Yotsuya-sanchome Station, right in Tokyo, and it's like a 5-minute walk from there. Next up, we have one of the coolest and most fun experiences I've probably ever had in Japan, and that is something called Yabusame. Back in the Kamakura period, they had this kind of archery on horseback that was used as training for the samurai. It's actually still practiced today at places like Canadian Camp Yatsugatake, which is where we went for our experience. Basically, you start off with a lesson on how to shoot the bows and then a quick lesson on how to ride the horse. Then, you get to try them together. Honestly, I just felt like a badass! It was quite challenging, especially maneuvering the bow, your fingers, and getting your fingers out of the way and all of that. But I never in my life thought that I'd get to experience archery on horseback — very, very cool. They’ll tailor the experience to you, so whether you're a professional rider or an absolute beginner, they'll choose the right horse for you. They also cater all of their teaching towards your level, so even if you're an absolute beginner, you'll still have a really, really great time. It'll cost 12,000 yen for the lesson, plus you must have purchased your own travel insurance from your home country. There's also an additional 4,400 yen for the traditional attire, which you don't have to wear, but the option is there if you want to. To get there, just catch the Limited Express train to Kabuchizawa Station, and then the club will send a private car to come and pick you up. Alternatively, you can rent a bike from the station and use that to ride to the club. If you do that, then from there, there's a bunch of other things in the area that you can see. We went to the Keith Haring Museum, and there's a lot of really famous architecture in the area as well. We also went to a little brewery that had... brew— sorry, it's really hard to say "brewery" — that had really delicious drinks. So, if you rent a bike, then you get to see all of that in the same area. So yeah, it's up to you! It depends on what you want to do with your day. Also, if you just so happen to be driving, there are three absolutely amazing spots that I can't not recommend. The first one is called Manami Inaga Lake. It's the most beautiful autumn leaves lake, and there are freaking swans swimming in it! It's so beautiful, but unfortunately, there's no public transport to get there. The second one is called Sakura Wanitsuka. This is basically just a photo location — you can't really do much here, but wow, what a photo! The last one is called Yamataka Jindai Zakura. This is a place that has the oldest cherry blossom tree in the world — question mark? Because no one really knows, but they say it's about 2,500 years old, which is insane. They also have a cherry blossom tree where they sent the seeds to space. When it came back, it started mutating! I've made a whole video about it. Oh my gosh, there's so much to see in this area. But yeah, again, these ones are a little trickier to get to, so only if you're driving. I've got so many recommendations! Oh, I could keep going. From Kabuchizawa, you're going to need to take the train back to Tachikawa Station anyway, so why not stay at the most beautiful hotel I've ever stayed at in Japan — Sorano Hotel. Sorano Hotel only opened a couple of years ago, and it is a beautiful modern take on Japanese Zen-style architecture. But the real standout feature here is the infinity pool on the top floor. The weather was kind of average when we were there, but on a clear day, you can see Mount Fuji, even in winter, because the pool is heated. It's a 10-minute walk from Tachikawa Station, and the price to stay is usually around 42,900 yen, but it can get a lot cheaper in the off-season. Unfortunately, there's an additional 2,000 yen to use the rooftop pool, but if you factor that into the price, it makes it a lot easier to stomach. Around the hotel, there are a bunch of really nice shops and restaurants. There's also a cinema that is doing something I've never seen before. They have something called "explosive sound," and sometimes they'll actually get the sound designer from the movie itself to come into the cinema and tweak the audio so that it sounds as close to the director's vision as possible. The movie we watched was Civil War, and it was so intense — so intense! Again, this is around a 10-minute walk from Tachikawa Station, and tickets are around 2,000 yen each. However, not every movie has the "explosive sound," so just double-check that when you make a reservation on the website. All the applicable links are in the description down below. Do you like Mount Fuji? Well, so do I! I don't like hiking Mount Fuji, though, because it's kind of boring. *Evil laughing* But I highly recommend hiking a mountain with a view of Mount Fuji. This one is probably the most easily accessible and the most doable within a day trip of Tokyo, and that is Mount Ishiwari. This mountain is just behind Lake Yamanaka, and the views of Mount Fuji are unreal. It’s about an 8 to 10 km hike, depending on where you finish, with only about 400 m of elevation gain. It will take most people between 4 to 5 hours without any breaks. Most people start their hike at Yamanakako Hirano Bus Stop, which is accessible with a direct bus from Shinjuku. The bus will cost you 2,350 yen and take 2 hours and 24 minutes. From there, the hike starts with a buttload of stairs and then just a regular incline. It’ll usually take you about an hour to get to the top of Mount Ishiwari. From there, some people will turn around and go back the same way, but I highly recommend you keep going down to Mount Hirao and then Mount Ohira. They've got the most vast, amazing, uninterrupted views of Mount Fuji that you'll ever see. To get down from here, you can take a number of different paths. It doesn’t really matter because you’ll probably finish somewhere near the lake, and there will be a bus that can pick you up along the lake anyway. Most people will catch the bus to Kawaguchiko Station and then take the train back to Tokyo, but it really depends on where you're going next. Some parts are pretty steep going up and down, so just be cautious of that. But overall, I think even if you’re a beginner hiker, as long as you’ve got a reasonable level of fitness, you’ll be totally fine. Please take caution, make sure you’ve got grippy shoes, bring at least a liter of water, and if the weather is bad, don’t go. Follow all of your basic hiking safety requirements. Especially in winter, be cautious because there might be snow. Overall, have fun, be safe, and don’t forget your camera! Next is an underrated — I'm going to say underappreciated — area of the city of Tokyo, and that is Kanda Jimbocho. This area has a long history of universities and schools nearby, so there are a lot of secondhand books here. Some are in English, but most are in Japanese. Even if you can’t read the Japanese, it’s kind of just interesting to look through. Some of these books have lived through multiple wars and are still held together. It’s wild. This area is also really famous for new and secondhand sports gear, so now you know where to go if you forgot to bring your goggles! In the area, we checked out two very, very interesting cafes and restaurants. The first one is called Aabi Café. This is like a board game café with heaps of games. A lot of them are in English. The really interesting part is that if all of the seats are taken up, you can just request to join somebody else’s group. So, yeah, if you go here, you might end up walking away with some new friends. The second place is something unlike any other experience I’ve ever had in my life. This is Vegetable Izakaya Ganja. I had no idea what we were walking into, so if you can keep it a surprise from your friends, I highly recommend that. This place is wild — maximalism doesn’t even begin to describe it. Decorations are everywhere, from the seats you sit on to the little squeaky toy you have to squeak to get the staff’s attention. Even the people working there are decked out head to toe. Afterwards, they come out and personally say goodbye to you. It was a very, very interesting experience. The food was good, but you’re not really there for the food. Clearly. They do have a lot of vegan options, which is great. I’d say overall it’s a little expensive for the food but very cheap for the experience you get. 10 out of 10 — can’t recommend this place enough! To get here, just go to Kanda or Jimbocho Station. Next up, we have a very relaxing, very chill day on the west side of Tokyo, and that is at Jindai Temple and the botanical gardens. Jindai sets itself apart with its very non-touristy, very chill vibe. They also have something called a goma daki ritual. This is where you write down your wish onto a plank of wood, give it to a monk, and they pray over it, burn it, and chant. It’s a very interesting, very unique experience. I wasn’t allowed to film it, but it’s almost meditative just to sit and listen to them. The ritual is held twice a day — 11:00 a.m. and 1:00 p.m. At least one person in your group has to buy the wooden plank if you want to participate, or you can just stand outside and watch for free. Overall, it’s just a really unique experience. Also in the area, there are a bunch of little shops and restaurants. They’re really famous for wasabi and soba, which were delicious. Just around the corner, we have the Jindai Botanical Gardens. This is a huge garden, and I’m shocked that I hadn’t heard of it before. They have probably the largest collection of the most interesting, unique, and sometimes rare flora from all around the world. It’s only 500 yen for entry. We were there in summer, so the main attraction was the rose garden. But if you come in spring, that’s probably the best time. The gardens are always changing and really dependent on the season. When we were there, they had a bonsai exhibition, which was really, really cool. I love bonsai! But yeah, it’s just a really lovely, relaxing area. Especially if you want to get away from the crowds and people, you’ll have so much space to yourself here. To get to Jindai, there are a number of different ways, but it usually always involves one train and one bus. I think we caught the train to Chofu Station and then took a bus to Jindai. Overall, it’s about an hour from Tokyo Station. And finally, we've got a beautiful, relaxing, natural retreat just two hours away from Tokyo at a place called Satologue. Japan, I think, is really going through a "sauna boom" at the moment. This is like my third sauna of the month, but this one is definitely the most beautiful. It’s in a brand-new facility, and they’ve thought of absolutely everything you could possibly need to make your experience worthwhile. They give you a little wellness shot before you start, and you get full use of the sauna. There's a cold plunge with water directly from the river, places to relax, and they give you a poncho and a hat. You also get a drink afterwards, as well as access to showers and all the amenities. Everything you could need is provided there. I don’t know, but I think it was something about the design of the building and the nature around the area that just made it feel genuinely relaxing. I felt very refreshed afterwards, and I really, really enjoyed it. It’s a little on the expensive side because it’s a luxury destination. For two people, it’ll cost you 22,000 yen for the half-day experience or 44,000 yen for the full day. You can add extra people, which will make it a bit cheaper depending on how many people are in your group. You may need to figure that out on their website. Definitely, I would save this for a special occasion or a romantic getaway or something like that. From Tachikawa Station, take two trains for about 1 hour and 25 minutes to Kori Station, and then from there, it’s a 14-minute walk to Satologue. All the details and everything are in the description down below. And that’s it! That is my list of 20 things in and around Tokyo you’ve probably never heard of. Have you ever heard of these places? Because I had never heard of them before making this video. So I hope they’re new, interesting, and exciting for you! Let me know what you think in the comments down below. Thank you so much to TCVB Tokyo Tourism for sponsoring this video. I had a really great time, so I hope you enjoyed watching it. Go follow me on Instagram, like and subscribe if you want to see more, and thank you for watching. I’ll see you guys next time. Bye!

videos.transcript: What's up, guys? We're here in Tokyo. In this video, we're going to show you our top things to do here. So, hope you enjoy it. Tokyo is a vibrant metropolis located on the central coast of Honshu, Japan's largest main island. As the country's bustling capital, the city blends futuristic skyscrapers and cutting-edge technology with serene temples. You'll also find world-class dining, a unique culture, and lush gardens here. We spent 10 days exploring every corner of this megacity to find its highlights and some hidden gems. In this video, we'll share the best things to do in Tokyo for a first-time visit, along with some essential travel tips at the end. Now, let's dive in. We are Zac and Ine. We make helpful videos about destinations around the world so you can easily plan your own adventures. Akihabara is a district located in central Tokyo. Known as the epicenter of Japanese pop culture, it's packed with multi-story arcades, massive electronic shops, maid cafes, and endless walls of anime and manga. It may be a sensory overload for some, but for tech lovers and pop culture fans, Akihabara is a great stop to hunt for rare collectibles and experience Japan's most eccentric side. Located in western Tokyo, Shibuya is arguably the city's most electric and chaotic district thanks to its massive crowds. At its center lies the world-famous Shibuya Crossing, where thousands of pedestrians cross the street from every direction each time the lights change. For the best perspective, watch the action from the nearby Starbucks. You can also see the crosswalk from a skyscraper such as Shabuya Sky, which we'll talk more about later in this video. Beyond the crossing, this district is also a hub for shopping, with the Nintendo store and Pokémon Center being popular spots to visit. But we especially enjoyed the Miyashita Park Mall, which had a unique rooftop setting. Located in the geographic heart of the city, the Imperial Palace serves as the primary residence of the Emperor of Japan. The inner palace buildings are strictly private, but you can visit the stunning East Gardens for free. There's three different entrances with the Ōte-mon Gate being the most popular and easiest to access. Built on the former site of Edo Castle, the grounds are protected by massive stone walls and you can still see the ruins of the original castle foundation. There's also a beautiful Japanese garden inside featuring a tranquil pond and seasonal flowers. Make sure to check the opening times in advance because the site is usually closed on Mondays and Fridays for maintenance. Ginza is an upscale district located in central Tokyo, just south of the Imperial Palace. This elegant neighborhood is renowned for its luxury boutiques, massive department stores, and high-end restaurants, making it a great place for shopping and soaking in the atmosphere. Beyond the designer labels, Ginza is home to the global flagship store of UNIQLO, a giant 12-story building featuring the world's largest selection of this iconic Japanese brand. If you visit on a weekend afternoon, the main street closes to cars and becomes a pedestrian paradise. Just a short walk from the upscale streets of Ginza, you can find the Tsukiji Outer market. While the wholesale inner market moved to Toyosu, the outer section remains a bustling maze of narrow alleys packed with traditional shops, sushi restaurants, and outdoor stalls offering everything from fresh sashimi and grilled octopus to wagyu skewers and seasonal fruits. Try to arrive somewhere between 8 and 10 in the morning as many vendors are closed by the early afternoon. Ueno Park is a vast cultural space situated in northern Tokyo, right next to the major transit hub of Ueno Station. This public park is famous for its world-class museums as well as its cherry blossoms, which draw massive crowds every spring. Besides art and nature, this park is deeply spiritual. We spent our morning here exploring a variety of stunning Buddhist temples and Shinto shrines. Located near the bustling Harajuku station, Meiji Jingu is one of Japan's most significant Shinto shrines. It honors Emperor Meiji and Emperor Shoken, the leaders who famously transitioned Japan from the age of the samurai into the modern world. Shintoism is Japan's ancient indigenous belief system, focusing on the worship of spirits called kami, which are found in nature. Thus, the Shrine can be found deep within a giant forest. This landmark is a must-visit for its grand wooden architecture and overall peacefulness. Be sure to look for the wall of sake barrels, which is a very popular photo spot. Located in the Tuyosu district, teamLab Planets is a digital art museum. This full body immersive exhibition invites you to walk through installations that stimulate all five senses. There's two main conceptual zones: the Garden and the Water area. We especially love the Water section, where you wade barefoot through a glowing koi pond and explore the infinite crystal universe. Another highlight was the falling universe of flowers where you can watch a cycle of blooms drift across the ceiling. If you can't get enough of this digital magic, Tokyo also hosts teamLab Borderless, offering a completely different digital immersion. Since these are some of the city's most sought-after attractions, you need to book tickets weeks in advance. This place is really popular, so if you want to jump the long queue, make sure you get a skip-the-line ticket, which brings us to the sponsor of today's video. GetYourGuide is the leading online travel experience platform with over 110,000 curated activities in 150 countries, from world-famous attractions to hidden gems. In Tokyo alone, you can choose from over 500 experiences, all rated and reviewed by verified travelers. Their user-friendly app keeps everything organized, from food tours to skip-the-line tickets. We personally used it for teamLab Planets, which allowed us to quickly move past the line and head straight inside. But we also booked an incredible sumo performance and a guided food tour that introduced us to the best of Japanese cuisine. We'll talk more about these activities a little later in this video. With 24/7 customer support and a flexible cancellation policy, GetYourGuide gives you the peace of mind to book confidently. The ability to modify plans up to 24 hours in advance ensures your itinerary can adapt to changing weather or preferences as well. Based on our experiences, we highly recommend GetYourGuide for your next adventure. Tokyo offers numerous observation decks to see the city from above, and we visited two very different spots. The first was Shibuya Sky, a popular open-air observatory that requires pre-booking your entry ticket a couple weeks ahead of time. Located on the rooftop of the Shibuya Scramble Square skyscraper, it offers an unobstructed view of the famous Shibuya Crossing and the sprawling Tokyo skyline. We also visited the Tokyo Metropolitan Government Building in Shinjuku, which offers a free indoor observation deck. While it has two towers you can ascend, we chose the North Tower since it had almost no waiting line. From here, you're more at the eye level of other massive skyscrapers, but the view is equally iconic. Other observatory options include the classic Tokyo Tower and Tokyo Skytree, the tallest structure in the city. Tokyo's themed cafés are a pretty wild scene, and it feels like there's a dedicated space for every interest imaginable. Takeshita Street in Harajuku is famous for its eclectic animal cafés, where you can interact with pigs, dogs, cats, owls, hedgehogs, and much more. However, we especially enjoyed the Avatar Robot Café DAWN, located further to the east in the Nihonbashi area. This permanent experimental space has a moving mission. It employs people who can't leave their homes due to medical or social reasons, who then remotely operate robot servers that bring coffee and food straight to your table. You can even talk to the remote human employees directly through the robots. For an easy day trip into nature, head west to Mount Takao. It's easily accessible by public transport with a 50-minute direct train ride on the Keio Line from Shinjuku Station to Takaosanguchi Station. The mountain offers a variety of hiking routes for all levels. We hiked up via trail 1, which is the most popular path in the area. This nicely paved trail takes you past an ancient temple. Once you reach the summit, you'll be rewarded with a stunning observation deck that offers views of Mount Fuji if it's a clear day. There's also a couple of small restaurants to enjoy a well-earned lunch near the viewpoint. To get back down, we decided to take the wooded trail number four to the chairlift, where we enjoyed a leisurely cruise to the base of the mountain. It was fairly quiet during our weekday visit, but it can get very busy with locals on weekends. You have to pay for the chairlift, but the mountain itself is free to enter. There's also some restaurants near the train station, which can be nice to visit before you head back to the city. Sumo is the national sport of Japan, deeply rooted in Shinto ritual and ancient tradition. While official professional tournaments are a highlight of the year, they only take place in Tokyo during January, May, and September, making tickets incredibly difficult to secure. Instead, we visited Tokyo Sumo Baya in Kiyosumi, a district famous for housing many stables where wrestlers live and train. We booked a 2-hour show which began with a fascinating dive into sumo history, followed by professional wrestlers demonstrating their skill in the ring. To top it off, visitors even had a chance to try out the moves for themselves. Tokyo is home to several traditional Japanese gardens that offer a peaceful escape from the city noise. We visited two of them, starting with Shinjuku. This giant park blends Japanese, English, and French styles. It costs 500 yen to enter and is famous for its pavilions, sprawling lawns, and seasonal blooms. There's also a beautiful greenhouse with many tropical and subtropical flowers. By contrast, Hamarikyu Gardens felt a little more off-the-beaten path. Located near Tokyo Bay, it features unique seawater ponds, picturesque bridges, and a charming tea house. You can find tranquility among the trimmed greenery with city skyscrapers as a backdrop. Located in the historic district of Asakusa, Senso-ji is Tokyo's oldest and most iconic Buddhist temple. You enter the temple grounds through the massive thunder gate which leads down Nakamise-Dori, a vibrant street packed with many traditional snacks and unique souvenirs. At the main hall, you can partake in rituals like burning incense for good health. We were there before sunset and it was fairly busy, so we'd recommend visiting early in the day or later at night if you want to avoid the biggest crowds. In the evening, the structures are beautifully illuminated. Shinjuku is located in the heart of western Tokyo and is a bustling neon-lit district filled with endless cafés and restaurants. We love staying in this area since we could find everything we could possibly need right at our doorstep. For a unique night out, you have to visit Golden Gai, a network of narrow alleys packed with tiny bars that offer a nostalgic taste of old Tokyo. Nearby is the famous Memory Lane, known for its smoky yakitori stalls. To truly experience the local flavors, we joined a food tour here and sampled 13 different Japanese dishes, including fresh sashimi, Okinawan sea grape salad, and a sweet fish-shaped cake filled with red beans. We left a link to the same tour we joined in the description below this video in case you want to do it yourself. As we promised at the start of the video, we'll now share three travel tips for Tokyo. First, selecting a place to stay depends on whether you want the city's vibrancy or its peace. Shinjuku and Shibuya are the ultimate central hubs where you can find thousands of restaurants, department stores, and large transit stations, making them the most convenient spots for exploring. For a more sophisticated or well-connected experience, Ueno and Ginza are excellent choices. Ginza offers luxury and proximity to Tokyo Station with easy bullet train access. On the other hand, Ueno has a direct express train link to the Narita airport. If you're seeking a peaceful residential escape, consider Ryogoku. It feels like a more local neighborhood with wider streets and a slower pace while remaining just a short train ride from the major sites. Having stayed in both Ryogoku and Shinjuku, we preferred the latter for its sheer wealth of food options right outside our door. The most efficient way to navigate Tokyo is to get your transit pass immediately. You can buy a physical Suica or Pasmo card in the station, but you almost always need to top it up with cash. Alternatively, you can buy it from your phone's digital wallet, allowing you to top up with your credit card. To make sense of the complex web of lines, we recommend using Google Maps. It provides specific details down to the exact platform number and the best station exit to use. This is especially vital in massive stations like Shinjuku, where an incorrect exit can lead to a 20-minute detour. Japanese public spaces are governed by a quiet and clean social etiquette. On trains and metros, this means keeping your voice at a whisper and making sure your phone is set to silent. This is because loud conversations are considered intrusive. Additionally, Japan has a unique approach to public consumption. You'll notice a lack of public trash cans, but the streets remain spotless because locals typically carry their litter home. Avoid eating or drinking while you walk. Instead, it's recommended to finish your snack at the same place you bought it, or at least just out front. Thanks for watching and we hope you enjoyed it. You can find more videos on our channel about Japan as well. These include other destinations and more tips for exploring the country. And if you're planning your own trip to Japan, we recommend keeping things organized with our interactive travel planner. Find a discount code in the description below the video. We included all the important links you'll need for your Tokyo experience in the description as well. We'll catch you on the next adventure.

videos.transcript: Welcome to Tokyo, a city where historic shrines sit beside ultramodern skyscrapers, where your next meal might come from a vending machine or a Michelin starred counter, and where mega crossings are just a few steps from peaceful side streets. Tokyo is iconic for its fast pace, but if you know where to look, it's also full of quiet moments, unexpected flavors, and unforgettable views. From trend setting streets to temple lined alleys, here's our travel guide for Tokyo, including some of our favorite spots and the best ways to explore them. Let's kick things off in the Shibuya district. It's the Tokyo you've probably imagined and then some. If there's one Tokyo moment you've seen everywhere online, it's probably Shibuya Crossing, one of the busiest pedestrian crossings in the world. The action peaks at dusk when waves of people move through the neon lit street. Or if you're visiting during the day, head upstairs to take in the spectacle from above. You'll find plenty of stellar vantage points, like this one from the top of Shibuya 109. For another epic viewpoint of the crossing and the rest of the city, take the elevator to Shibuya Sky. This observation deck offers 360° views across the city. If you're traveling during spring high season, book your tickets ahead online to skip the lines. If you're looking for food in Shibuya, follow the scent of Char Grilled Skewers and squeeze into a yakitori stall in Nonbei Yokōcho. This narrow alleyway near Shibuya Station is lined with lantern-lit bars where locals unwind over late night bites. Next, let's head north to Shinjuku, a neighborhood of extremes. Fast-paced and serene, gritty and refined, old school and ultramodern, all packed into one dynamic district. Start outside Shinjuku Station where you'll be welcomed by a giant 3D calico cat that blinks and stretches above the crowds. Then head into Omoide Yokōcho, for smoky grills, paper lanterns, and tiny food stalls. It's the perfect spot for cheap eats and big flavors. Just remember to bring cash. Many of the stalls are proudly low tech. Need a quiet moment? Just a short walk away is one of Tokyo's most famous green spaces, Shinjuku Gyoen. Or visit Hanazono Shrine, a pocket of calm tucked between office towers and arcades. Ring the bell for good luck and tie a fortune beneath the Torii gates. When night falls, the nearby Golden Gai lights up with glowing signs and cozy bars. They may be compact, but each one overflows with character. Continue your nightlife adventure just two subway stops west, where Tokyo Night & Light transforms the Metropolitan Government Building into a mind-boggling display every 30 minutes. Next up is Harajuku, where Tokyo's cool, creative edge collides with tradition. You'll find high fashion, pop culture, as well as leafy parks, and one of the city's most sacred shrines, all packed into a few walkable blocks. Start with Takeshita Street. This narrow pedestrian lane is packed with pop culture boutiques and all kinds of snacks. For a complete contrast, stroll over to Omotesandō Avenue. It's a treeline stretch of modern design and luxury storefronts. One of the highlights here is the mirrored entryway at Tokyo Plaza. When you're ready to take it down a notch, take the short walk to Yoyogi Park, just around the corner. Wide lawns and shaded paths make it one of Tokyo's best places to relax. It's a calm open space that feels worlds away from Takeshita's buzz. Tucked in the forest just a few steps away is Meiji Shrine, one of Tokyo's most important Shinto sites. The towering Torii gate is made from ancient Cyprus, and the walk to the main hall feels like stepping into another world. Next up is Ueno, a neighborhood that connects you to Tokyo's roots, with centuries old temples, national museums, and lively markets that balance the city's fast pace with culture and tradition. And it's all centered around one of the city's most beloved parks. Ueno Park is a sprawling green space that's home to museums, shrines, and shady spots for picnicking or people watching. If you're traveling in spring, it's one of Tokyo's most iconic cherry blossom spots, but it's gorgeous all year round. Within the park itself, you can explore the Tokyo National Museum, where ancient art, Buddhist sculptures, and centuries old scrolls trace Japan's legacy. For quieter moments, step over to Bentendō Temple or visit Gojōten Shrine. And at the heart of the park stands Ueno Tōshōgū Shrine. with golden details and a lantern-lined path leading to its gates, it's a rare glimpse of Edo‑era craftsmanship right in the middle of the city. When you get hungry, head just outside the park to Ameyoko Market, one of Tokyo's liveliest shopping streets. Next up is Akihabara, the city's tech capital. This neighborhood is packed with electronics, anime, and arcades stacked floor by floor. Manderake is a must visit for collectors stocked floor to ceiling with comic books, figurines, and rare finds. Nearby, Super Potato is retro gaming heaven. With dusty cartridges, pixel art, and vintage consoles, it will transport you straight back to the '90s. Or if you prefer to try your luck, Akihabara's got an endless lineup of gacha pon halls. Rows and rows of capsule machines with surprises ranging from cute to quirky. When you need a quick bite between battles, grab some Kushiyaki skewers. Tender grilled bites of meat and veg on a stick. Once you've had your fill of flashing lights and capsule toys, head east to the neighborhood where tradition still thrives. Step into Asakusa and you step into Old Tokyo. Here, tradition lives on in every gate, lantern, and curl of incense smoke. Start your journey at Kaminari mon, the iconic red thunder gate that marks the entrance to Asakusa. From here, walk straight down Nakamise Dori, a centuries old shopping street lined with stalls selling snacks and souvenirs. You'll want to try the sweet Ningyo Yaki cakes made of batter poured into cute molds. At the end of the street, you'll reach Sensō-ji, Tokyo's oldest temple. Cleanse your hands in the dragon fountain and draw a fortune from the omikuji stand. Tucked just beside the temple, Asakusa shrine offers a peaceful pause shaded by trees and watched over by lion guardians. It's a great place to slow down and take it all in. For more fresh air, head to the prominade along the Sumida River. It's a short walk from Sensō-ji and a peaceful stretch where locals stroll and boats drift by. Just across the river, Tokyo Skytree rises from the surrounding buildings. Head to the top just before sunset for one of Tokyo's most unforgettable views. Tokyo is a city of contrast, and each neighborhood tells a different story. You can start your day in a centuries old shrine and end it under a sky of glowing lights. Whether you come for tradition, tech, food, or fashion, there's a Tokyo for every vibe. So, what's yours? Save this video for your Japan trip and tell us which neighborhood you're going to explore first.
